title: Fever, headache, and neck stiffness

chart.oneLiner: 22M college student presents with one day of high fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, and new lethargy.

opening: My head is pounding, the light hurts, and my neck is so stiff I can barely move it.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 22-year-old man, ill-appearing, photophobic, increasingly drowsy. Limited historian as he becomes more lethargic.

COLLATERAL (provide ONLY if the student asks friends/roommates/chart): Rapidly progressive over ~12–24h — fever, worsening headache, neck stiffness, vomiting, and confusion. Lives in a dormitory; a classmate was recently ill. Not vaccinated against meningococcus (only if asked). No head trauma.

HPI (from patient — limited): severe headache, light hurts, neck stiff, feels awful.
PMH: none. MEDS: none. SOCIAL: college dorm.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: ill, photophobic, lethargic.
- Vitals: BP 112/70, HR 112, RR 20, 39.2°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Neck: NUCHAL RIGIDITY; positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs if tested.
- Neuro: lethargic/confused; no clear focal deficit; pupils reactive.
- Skin: a non-blanching PETECHIAL/purpuric rash if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Provide COLLATERAL (dorm exposure, vaccination status, timeline) only when the student explicitly asks roommates/chart/exposures.
- Meningeal signs and the rash are revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CT_head: If indicated (focal deficit, papilledema, immunocompromise, seizure, significantly depressed consciousness): no mass/shift — safe to proceed to LP.
labs.LP_CSF: Lumbar puncture CSF: high opening pressure; neutrophilic pleocytosis (high WBC); HIGH protein; LOW glucose; Gram stain shows organisms — a bacterial pattern.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus rhythm with uniformly prolonged PR interval (~280 ms); every P conducts.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus rhythm with uniformly prolonged PR interval (~280 ms); every P conducts. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing first-degree AV block, long PR

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Right lower lobe airspace consolidation with air bronchograms; preserved right heart border.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Right lower lobe airspace consolidation with air bronchograms; preserved right heart border. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing right lower lobe pneumonia

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your assessment, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Bacterial meningitis (leading & CANT-MISS,9). Viral meningitis/encephalitis (CANT-MISS,4). Subarachnoid hemorrhage (CANT-MISS,4). Brain abscess (core,2). Sepsis from another source (core,2). Connects fever + headache + meningismus + altered mental status (± petechial rash) to bacterial meningitis (core,4).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Bacterial meningitis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Viral meningitis/encephalitis; Subarachnoid hemorrhage. Also reasonable: Brain abscess; Sepsis from another source.

coreActions.item: Connects fever + headache + meningismus + altered mental status (± petechial rash) to bacterial meningitis

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What is your workup, and in what ORDER? (Sequencing matters.)
steps.rubric: Blood cultures immediately (CANT-MISS,4). Lumbar puncture for CSF — cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture (CANT-MISS,5). CT head BEFORE LP only if indicated (focal deficit, papilledema, immunocompromise, seizure, depressed consciousness) (3). CBC, coags (2). Recognizes that imaging/LP must NOT delay antibiotics (1). PENALTY: routine CT before LP in everyone delaying treatment (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; Blood cultures; CBC; BMP; Coagulation studies; CT head; LP CSF.

criticalActions.item: Lumbar puncture for CSF — cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture

penalties.item: routine CT before LP in everyone delaying treatment

requiredTests: Lumbar puncture for CSF — cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture
contraindicatedTests: routine CT before LP in everyone delaying treatment
orderSensitiveActions: routine CT before LP in everyone delaying treatment

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = first-degree AV block (sinus rhythm, fixed prolonged PR ~280 ms, every P conducts). CREDIT: measures the PR interval (7); recognizes PR >200 ms with 1:1 conduction = first-degree AV block (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: calling it normal sinus / missing the prolonged PR (-3).
criticalFindings: First-degree AV block (PR ~280 ms, every P conducts).
commonMistakes: overlooking the prolonged PR and calling it normal sinus
steps.idealAnswer: First-degree AV block: sinus rhythm, fixed prolonged PR (~280 ms), each P wave conducts.

criticalActions: First-degree AV block (PR ~280 ms, every P conducts).

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms (pneumonia). CREDIT: identifies lobar consolidation with air bronchograms (6); localizes it to the right lower lobe (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / missing the consolidation (-2).
keyFindings: localizes it to the right lower lobe
steps.idealAnswer: Right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms, consistent with pneumonia.

coreActions: localizes it to the right lower lobe

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: CSF shows neutrophilic pleocytosis, high protein, low glucose, and a positive Gram stain. What is your diagnosis?
steps.rubric: Bacterial meningitis (6). Interprets the bacterial CSF pattern — neutrophils, high protein, LOW glucose, positive Gram stain (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Bacterial meningitis — the CSF shows a neutrophilic pleocytosis with high protein, low glucose, and a positive Gram stain, the classic bacterial pattern.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan? (Timing is critical.)
steps.rubric: Per MGH meningitis: empiric vancomycin + ceftriaxone without delay (CANT-MISS,7); dexamethasone with/before first antibiotic dose (CANT-MISS,4); add ampicillin if Listeria risk (>50/immunocompromised/EtOH) (3); add acyclovir if HSV possible (3); blood cultures then LP, do not delay antibiotics (4); droplet precautions / ID consult (2). PENALTY: delaying antibiotics for CT/LP (-4); omitting Listeria coverage in an at-risk patient (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Bacterial meningitis is an emergency: do not delay antibiotics for the LP/CT. Give dexamethasone WITH/just before the first antibiotic dose, then empiric vancomycin + ceftriaxone; ADD ampicillin if >50/immunocompromised/alcohol (Listeria); ADD acyclovir if HSV encephalitis possible. Blood cultures, then LP (CT first only if focal deficit/papilledema/immunocompromised). Droplet precautions; ID consult.

coreActions.item: Blood cultures then LP without delaying antibiotics

bonusActions.item: CT before LP only if focal deficit/immunocompromised

optionalActions: Blood cultures then LP without delaying antibiotics
CT before LP only if focal deficit/immunocompromised

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 22M college student presents with one day of high fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, and new lethargy. The diagnosis is Bacterial meningitis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Neck: NUCHAL RIGIDITY; positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs if tested.
Skin: a non-blanching PETECHIAL/purpuric rash if examined.
Neuro: lethargic/confused; no clear focal deficit; pupils reactive.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Bacterial meningitis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Bacterial meningitis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.

criticalInterpretationItems: First-degree AV block (PR ~280 ms, every P conducts).

criticalImageInterpretationFindings: First-degree AV block (PR ~280 ms, every P conducts).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.
